title: Submit Your Questions: Inside The World of Online Romance Scams
gist: A promotional announcement for WIRED's first Book Club livestream (July 16), featuring NYT journalist Carlos Barragan on his book 'The Yahoo Boys,' which embeds with Lagos-based Nigerian romance scammers to document how the internet fuels large-scale online deception.

insights.claim: Nigeria's 'Yahoo Boys' are an organized cohort of young grifters operating out of Lagos who run online romance scams as a livelihood, documented via embedded first-hand reporting.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Signals a maturing, geographically concentrated online-fraud industry worth tracking for cybersecurity, fintech fraud-prevention, and identity-verification exposure.
insights.quote: Barragan, a journalist and researcher at The New York Times, flew to Lagos to embed himself with a group of young, desperate grifters.
insights.locator: For our very first WIRED Book Club

insights.claim: The book frames online romance scams as an internet-enabled engine of mass emotional and financial harm rather than isolated con artistry.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Reinforces the investment case for scam-detection, dating-platform trust-and-safety, and payments fraud tooling as structural, not episodic, demand.
insights.quote: The account he brings back is a funny, sad, enraging read about how the internet can fuel heartbreak.
insights.locator: For our very first WIRED Book Club

insights.claim: WIRED is gating its Book Club livestreams behind a paid subscription, using live author events (with post-event replays) as a subscriber-retention benefit.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Illustrates the media playbook of converting live/exclusive programming into subscription value—relevant for assessing Conde Nast and digital-media monetization trends.
insights.quote: This livestream is reserved as a subscriber benefit.

insights.claim: WIRED's coverage priorities now explicitly center on prediction markets, the future of media, and how AI is changing the internet, per its lead Book Club writer's beat.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Prediction markets being named as a core tech-journalism beat is a signal the category is entering mainstream investor and media attention.
insights.quote: senior writer at WIRED, covering prediction markets, the future of media, and how AI is changing the internet
